title: What is a package
instruction:
Every Go file starts with a `package` declaration. It tells Go which package this file belongs to.

`package main` is special — it is the entry point. Every runnable Go program needs exactly one `package main` with a `func main()`.

All other packages are libraries. You write them to share code. You import them to use other people's code.

Run this. It imports two packages from the standard library and uses both.
successMessage: Two packages imported, two jobs done. fmt handled printing, strings handled the text manipulation. That is the package system in one example.

title: Importing standard library packages
instruction:
Go's standard library is huge. You do not install anything — it comes with Go.

Some of the most useful packages:
- `fmt` — formatted I/O
- `strings` — string manipulation
- `math` — mathematical functions
- `time` — dates and durations
- `os` — operating system interaction
- `strconv` — string↔number conversion

Add two more `fmt.Println` lines that use `strings.Contains` to check if `sentence` contains `"Go"` and `strings.Count` to count how many times `"o"` appears.
expectedOutput: GO IS THE BEST LANGUAGE TO LEARN

codeChecks.message: Use strings.Contains(sentence, "Go") to check if it contains "Go".
Use strings.Count(sentence, "o") to count occurrences of "o".
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Contains → true. Count → 3 (language, to, Go). The strings package has 20+ functions like these. You never need to write string manipulation from scratch in Go.

title: The strings package
instruction:
The `strings` package handles almost every text operation you will ever need.

Run this to see six common functions in action. Then add one more line: use `strings.ReplaceAll` to replace every space in `sentence` with a `-` and print the result.

codeChecks.message: Use strings.ReplaceAll(sentence, " ", "-") to replace spaces.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Seven functions, zero manual loops. The strings package is one of the most used in all of Go. Keep it in your mental toolkit.

title: The math package
instruction:
The `math` package gives you mathematical constants and functions.

`math.Pi` — the value of π
`math.Sqrt(x)` — square root
`math.Pow(x, y)` — x to the power y
`math.Abs(x)` — absolute value
`math.Round(x)` — round to nearest integer

Add three lines: print the square root of 144, 2 to the power of 10, and the absolute value of -7.5.

codeChecks.message: Print math.Sqrt(144).
Print math.Pow(2, 10).
Print math.Abs(-7.5).
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: √144=12, 2¹⁰=1024, |-7.5|=7.5. The math package handles every standard mathematical operation. No need to implement these yourself.

title: The time package
instruction:
The `time` package handles dates, times, durations, and formatting.

`time.Now()` — current time
`time.Since(t)` — how long since `t`
`t.Format("2006-01-02")` — format a time (Go uses a reference date, not strftime)

Run this. The reference date `2006-01-02 15:04:05` is Go's fixed reference — always use those specific numbers for formatting.

Then add a line that prints today's date in `DD/MM/YYYY` format.

codeChecks.message: Use now.Format("02/01/2006") to print in DD/MM/YYYY format.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Go's time formatting is unique — instead of %d %m %Y, you use the reference date Jan 2 15:04:05 2006. Weird at first, but you will never forget 2006-01-02.

title: The os package
instruction:
The `os` package lets you interact with the operating system: environment variables, command-line arguments, file system, exit codes.

`os.Getenv("KEY")` — read an environment variable
`os.Args` — command-line arguments slice
`os.Exit(1)` — exit with a non-zero code (signals failure)

Run this. It reads the `HOME` environment variable (or `USERPROFILE` on Windows). Then add a line that prints how many arguments are in `os.Args`.

codeChecks.message: Print the count: fmt.Println("Args count:", len(os.Args))
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: os.Args[0] is always the program name itself — so even with no extra arguments, len(os.Args) is 1. The os package is your bridge between Go and the machine it runs on.

title: Exported vs unexported
instruction:
In Go, whether something is public or private is decided by a single rule:

**Capital first letter = exported (usable outside the package)**
**Lowercase first letter = unexported (private to the package)**

This applies to functions, types, variables, and struct fields.

`fmt.Println` is exported — `Println` starts with a capital P.
`strings.toUpper` would be unexported — but Go named it `ToUpper`.

Run this. Then fix the code by changing `greet` to `Greet` and `person` struct fields to start with capitals.
hint: Change greet to Greet, name to Name, age to Age in both the struct definition and usage.
expectedOutput: Hello, Alex! You are 25 years old.

codeChecks.message: Capitalise the type name: type Person struct
Capitalise the field: Name string
Capitalise the field: Age int
Capitalise the function: func Greet(...)

successMessage: One rule, zero keywords. No public, no private, no protected — just the case of the first letter. Simple and consistent throughout every Go project ever written.

title: What is a module
instruction:
A module is a collection of Go packages with a shared identity and version.

Every Go project has a `go.mod` file that defines:
- The module path (like `github.com/yourname/yourproject`)
- The Go version
- External dependencies

You create one with: `go mod init module-name`

Run this. It reads `runtime.Version()` to show the Go version your code is running on — the same version recorded in go.mod.
successMessage: Your program knows what runtime it is on. The runtime package exposes this and more — goroutine counts, memory stats, CPU cores. The module system ties it all together into a versioned, shareable project.

title: Using a third-party package
instruction:
Beyond the standard library, Go's ecosystem has thousands of packages at `pkg.go.dev`.

You add one with:
```
go get github.com/some/package
```

This updates `go.mod` and creates `go.sum` (a lockfile with checksums).

For this tutorial environment we can only use the standard library — but the pattern is identical. Here we use `encoding/json` (standard library) the same way you would use any external package: import, call, done.

Run this. See how json.Marshal turns a Go struct into JSON bytes.
successMessage: A Go struct became JSON in three lines. This is how every Go API serializes responses. The pattern — import, call, handle error — is identical whether it is a standard library package or a third-party one.

title: Import aliases
instruction:
You can give an imported package a different name using an alias:

```
import (
    str "strings"
    m   "math"
)
```

Then use `str.ToUpper()` and `m.Sqrt()` instead of the full names.

Aliases are also used to resolve naming conflicts when two packages have the same last segment.

Run this — it uses aliases for both `strings` and `math`. Then add a line using the `m` alias to print `math.Pi` rounded to 2 decimal places.

codeChecks.message: Use m.Pi (the math alias) to access Pi.
Format Pi to 2 decimal places with %.2f.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Aliases keep imports readable when names get long or conflict. You will see them most often with database drivers and generated code.

title: Blank import
instruction:
Sometimes you import a package purely for its side effects — it registers something when it loads, but you never call its functions directly.

`import _ "some/package"` — the blank identifier suppresses the "imported and not used" error.

The most common example: database drivers. `import _ "github.com/lib/pq"` registers PostgreSQL as a driver with `database/sql` without you calling anything from `pq` directly.

Run this. The `image/png` package is blank-imported to register the PNG decoder.
expectedOutput: PNG support registered via blank import
successMessage: The blank import ran the png package's init() function — registering the PNG decoder — without polluting your code with unused identifiers. You will use this pattern with database drivers and image formats.

title: Build a string toolkit
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a small text analysis tool using `strings`, `fmt`, and `math`:

1. Start with this sentence: `"  the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog  "`
2. Trim the whitespace
3. Print the trimmed sentence in UPPERCASE
4. Split it into words and print the word count
5. Print whether it contains `"fox"`
6. Print the average word length rounded to 2 decimal places

Expected output:
```
THE QUICK BROWN FOX JUMPS OVER THE LAZY DOG
Word count: 9
Contains fox: true
Avg word length: 3.89
```
hint: For average length: loop through words, sum all len(word), divide by len(words). Use math.Round or %.2f for formatting.
expectedOutput: THE QUICK BROWN FOX JUMPS OVER THE LAZY DOG

codeChecks.message: Use strings.TrimSpace to trim the whitespace.
Use strings.ToUpper to print in uppercase.
Use strings.Split to split into words.
Use strings.Contains to check for "fox".
Use math package or %.2f to format the average to 2 decimal places.

successMessage: Five packages worth of functionality in under 20 lines. Text trimmed, uppercased, split, searched, and analysed. The standard library does the heavy lifting — you just orchestrate it.
